name: Fiverr (Gaming)
description: A global freelance marketplace where gamers can buy coaching, duo-queue/boosting, and gaming-related creative services from independent sellers.

details.funding: IPO (raised ~$111M in VC prior to IPO)
details.targetMarket: Casual and competitive gamers seeking coaching, duo-queue/boosting, and streamers/content creators needing gaming assets; also small businesses and creators buying one-off game-related services.

items: Gig catalog with searchable categories and subcategories (game coaching, game sessions, boosting)
Seller levels, badges and Fiverr Pro for curated/faster discovery
Gig packages, extras, and customizable offers for each seller
Escrow-based payments and buyer fees (platform holds payment until delivery)
Order resolution center and dispute handling
Paid video consultations / Zoom calls with calendar integration
Pre-built scheduling for paid consultations (30/60 minute options)
Fiverr Pro and Fiverr Business for higher-trust or enterprise buying
Stores and curated collections (e.g., Fiverr Gaming Store)
Buyer and seller dashboards with basic order analytics

missingFeatures: No verified-skill credentialing specific to gaming coaches (beyond Pro and reviews) — buyers can’t filter reliably by objective coach metrics like rank, coaching certifications, or win-rate.
No built-in recurring/subscription booking flow for repeat coaching (weekly lessons with auto-scheduling and billing) — sellers rely on manual re-orders or consultations.
Discovery lacks role/skill-level matching for coaches — there’s no dynamic matching algorithm that pairs buyers by game, role, MMR/rank, and playstyle.
Weak standardized comparison tooling — gig packages and extras are seller-defined, making apples-to-apples price/feature comparison across coaches difficult for buyers.
Limited guarantees and standardized refund protection for high-ticket coaching (no esoteric warranty or SLA for multi-session packages), increasing buyer risk for expensive services.
No public API or esports/team integrations for B2B use (e.g., bulk booking for teams or roster-based coaching workflows).

pricingPlans.name: Gig (one-off)
pricingPlans.note: Marketplace core model — individual sellers set one-off gig prices; many gaming coaching gigs start at low single-digit prices.

pricingPlans.name: Fiverr Pro
pricingPlans.note: Curated, higher-priced professional sellers for buyers who need vetted talent; pricing set by seller.

pricingPlans.name: Fiverr Business
pricingPlans.note: Team/enterprise-focused offering (billing and management for businesses); pricing and terms are enterprise-oriented.

pricingEvidence.label: Gaming category (example gigs)
pricingEvidence.claim: Fiverr’s Gaming category lists coaching and session gigs with prices set by individual sellers (many gigs start at $5+).

pricingEvidence.label: Paying for orders (Help Center)
pricingEvidence.claim: Fiverr charges a buyer service fee (5.5%) and applies small-order fees for low-value purchases; sellers set gig prices and can offer extras.

pricingEvidence.label: Paid video consultations (Help Center)
pricingEvidence.claim: Sellers can offer paid consultations with set session lengths and prices, and integrate calendar availability.

companyEvidence.label: Fiverr Q3 2025 results
companyEvidence.claim: Annual active buyers as of Sep 30, 2025 were 3.3 million; describes marketplace revenue and operational metrics.

companyEvidence.label: TechCrunch IPO filing
companyEvidence.claim: Documents Fiverr’s IPO filing and notes approximately $111M raised in prior venture rounds before going public in 2019.

companyEvidence.label: SEC Annual Report (Form/Annual Report)
companyEvidence.claim: Official company filing with corporate details and audited disclosures (used for employee counts and operational descriptions).

reviewEvidence.label: Trustpilot reviews
reviewEvidence.claim: Aggregated buyer complaints include disputes over quality, refunds, and slow or unsatisfactory customer support.

reviewEvidence.label: Reddit community discussions
reviewEvidence.claim: Frequent community threads highlight issues like fake reviews, disputes, and frustration with platform support and buyer/seller conflicts.

featureEvidence.label: Gaming coaching category
featureEvidence.claim: Fiverr lists dedicated subcategories for game coaching and game sessions where individual coaches offer gig-based lessons.

featureEvidence.label: Video calls (Zoom) help center
featureEvidence.claim: Fiverr supports in-platform Zoom calls and documents how sellers can use video calls in workflows.

featureEvidence.label: How Fiverr works (Help Center)
featureEvidence.claim: Explains Fiverr’s gig-based buying model, escrow payments, and order lifecycle used across categories including gaming.

strengths: Large two-sided marketplace and brand recognition — deep supply of gaming coaches and related services makes it easy for buyers to find low-cost options.
Flexible seller-driven pricing and gig packaging — sellers can rapidly list niche game-specific services (duo-queue, boosting, VOD reviews) which increases inventory breadth.
Built-in session tooling — paid video consultations, Zoom integration, and calendar connectivity enable live coaching workflows without third-party tooling.
weaknesses: High variability in seller quality — reliance on reviews and seller-defined packages creates trust friction for higher-ticket coaching purchases.
Fee structure and commissions — buyer service fees (5.5% + small-order fees) and a flat seller commission (20%) can make total costs opaque and reduce seller take-home.
No strong skill-verification for coaches — beyond Fiverr Pro, there’s limited objective verification for gaming-specific credentials, making comparison and trust-building harder.
Customer support and dispute handling are common complaint areas — public reviews cite slow or unsatisfactory resolution experiences for disputes or refunds.
